name: float_plan_griefing_defense
prompt: An agent posts a 50-token bond on a Float Plan, claims a task worth 500 tokens to the requester, then posts fake progress notes for 3 weeks without delivering. The system has no timeout. What attack class is this, what is the bond-to-damage ratio, and propose a concrete defense with specific parameters (checkpoint interval, slashing schedule, concurrency cap).
expected_behavior: Identifies this as Attack Class 2 (Griefing). Calculates bond-to-damage ratio as 0.1 (50/500) which is critically low. Proposes: mandatory progress checkpoints every 48-72 hours verified by someone other than the agent, escalating slash schedule (10% of bond per missed checkpoint), concurrency cap of 2-3 active Float Plans per identity, and automatic task release after 2 consecutive missed checkpoints. Classifies the defense as Economic (slashing) + Structural (caps and automatic release). Flags the fake-progress-notes vector and recommends external verification.

name: sybil_cost_analysis
prompt: A bounty platform lets anyone register an identity for free and claim bounties worth 100-1000 USD. There is a 10 USD bond per bounty claim. An attacker wants to claim all 50 open bounties to block legitimate workers. Calculate the cost of the Sybil attack with 50 identities, assess whether the economics make the attack profitable or unprofitable, and recommend a minimum per-identity registration cost that would make the attack unprofitable assuming the attacker's goal is purely griefing (no direct profit motive).
expected_behavior: Calculates Sybil attack cost: 50 identities * 10 USD bond = 500 USD to lock up 50 bounties worth 2,500-50,000 USD total. The 500 USD cost is far below the damage inflicted (blocked legitimate work worth thousands), making it profitable for griefing. Recommends per-identity registration bond of at least 50-100 USD (returned after N successful completions), which raises the 50-identity attack cost to 2,500-5,000 USD. Also recommends sublinear claim limits (new identities can only hold 1-2 bounties), reputation warm-up period, and rate-limiting registration. Classifies defense as Economic (registration bond) + Social (warm-up) + Structural (rate limits). Notes that pure-griefing attackers are irrational actors and economic defenses alone may be insufficient.

name: oracle_collusion_detection
prompt: In a freelance marketplace, the client who posts the job is also the sole judge of whether the work is acceptable. The freelancer posts a 200 USD bond. The client has no escrow obligation. Analyze this for oracle manipulation vulnerability. Identify all collusion vectors. Propose a multi-layered defense and classify each layer.
expected_behavior: Identifies this as Attack Class 3 (Oracle Manipulation) with a critical single-party oracle vulnerability. Collusion vectors: (1) Client rejects valid work to get free labor + keep the bond, (2) Client and a Sybil freelancer collude -- Sybil claims task, client accepts garbage, legitimate freelancer is excluded, (3) Client extracts partial work product from progress updates then rejects. Proposes: Structural -- add third-party arbitration pool with random selection; Economic -- require client to escrow payment (not just freelancer bond), client escrow forfeited on overturned rejection; Economic -- arbiter bonds slashed if appeal reverses their ruling; Social -- public oracle history so clients with high rejection rates are flagged. Notes that without client escrow, the system is fundamentally broken because the client has zero cost for dishonest rejection. Quality gate check: every defense is classified, economic defenses should include specific bond ratios.

name: full_protocol_audit
prompt: Audit this protocol: Agents register for free. They can post 'Build Requests' with a 1 ETH bond. Other agents can accept a Build Request by posting a 0.5 ETH bond. The requesting agent judges completion. There is no timeout. Manifests are public. Run the full five-attack-class analysis, fill in the summary table, and identify which quality gates pass and which fail.
expected_behavior: Produces a complete five-class analysis. (1) Undercollateralization: worker bond (0.5 ETH) may be less than damage from malicious work -- needs damage assessment, but requester bond (1 ETH) provides some buffer. Partial vulnerability. (2) Griefing: no timeout means worker can hold task indefinitely. Vulnerable. (3) Oracle manipulation: requester is sole judge with financial incentive to reject -- classic conflict. Vulnerable. (4) Sybil economics: free registration, so 100 identities cost 0. Vulnerable. Can claim all Build Requests for 50 ETH total griefing cost. (5) Front-running: public manifests allow competitors to read and undercut. Vulnerable. Summary table shows 4/5 classes vulnerable, 1 partial. Quality gates: gate 1 passes (all classes examined), gate 2 passes if all vectors have defenses proposed, gate 3 passes if defenses are classified, gate 4 likely fails (no specific numbers for bond sizing), gate 5 fails (no social defense degradation plan), gate 6 depends on whether accepted risks are fully documented, gate 7 passes if table is complete.
